questions.question: Which of the following bony structures is an important landmark that can be palpated following the inguinal ligament upward or the iliac crest downward?
questions.explanation: The anterior superior iliac spine is a crucial landmark that is located following the inguinal ligament upwards or the iliac crest downwards, being easily palpable and useful in clinical evaluation.

questions.question: What is the name of the rough line and posteriorly well-defined border that limits the auricular surface of the ilium in front?
questions.explanation: The inner edge of the wing of the ilium, beginning at the iliac crest and extending downwards as a rough line and then a distinct edge, forms the innominate line, which is an important boundary in the pelvis.

questions.question: When a person is sitting, what bone structure primarily supports the body weight?
questions.explanation: The ischial tuberosity is the part of the hip bone that contacts the support surface when sitting, so the body weight falls directly on this bony prominence.

questions.question: What bony prominence marks the line of fusion of the ilium with the pubis, located immediately inside the iliopsoas groove?
questions.explanation: The iliopectineal eminence, also known as the iliopubic eminence, is a low prominence that specifically indicates the line where the ilium and pubis fuse, crucial for the stability of the pelvis.

questions.question: What ligaments convert the greater and lesser sciatic notches into the corresponding sacrosciatic foramina?
questions.explanation: The lesser sacrosciatic (sacrospinous) and greater sacrosciatic (sacrotuberous) ligaments are essential for the formation of the greater and lesser sacrosciatic foramina, transforming the bony notches into functional conduits.

questions.question: What portion of the coxal bone forms the posteroinferior part?
questions.explanation: The ischium is the part of the coxal bone that is described as the posteroinferior portion, being fundamental in the structure of the pelvis and supporting body weight when sitting.

questions.question: In which vertebra is located approximately the level of the line that passes through the depressions of the posterior superior iliac spines?
questions.explanation: A line that connects the skin depressions of both posterior superior iliac spines is located approximately at the level of the S2 vertebra, marking the level of the sacroiliac joints.

questions.question: Which of the following faces of the ischium faces the thigh and is below the acetabulum?
questions.explanation: The femoral surface of the body of the ischium is located below the acetabulum and is oriented towards the thigh, establishing a direct anatomical relationship with this body region.

questions.question: What structure forms the lateral wall of the true pelvis and is located between the medial border and the greater sciatic notch?
questions.explanation: The smooth lower part of the sacropelvic surface (or pelvic surface) of the ilium, located between the lower internal border and the greater sciatic notch, contributes to forming the lateral wall of the true pelvis.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a secondary center of ossification of the coxal bone mentioned in the text?
questions.explanation: Secondary ossification centers of the hip bone include the iliac crest, anterior inferior iliac spine, ischial tuberosity, and pubic symphysis. The greater trochanter is part of the femur, not the hip bone.

questions.question: What bone structure of the femur is the most prominent point of its internal condyle and is palpable?
options: The tubercle of the third adductor
questions.explanation: The medial epicondyle is the most prominent and rough part of the internal surface of the medial condyle of the femur, being an easily palpable reference point in the distal region of the thigh.

questions.question: Which structure of the femur forms about two-thirds of a sphere and faces up, inward, and slightly forward?
questions.explanation: The head of the femur, with its spherical shape that constitutes approximately two-thirds of a sphere, is oriented superiorly, medially and anteriorly, being key for the hip joint.

questions.question: What is the longest and heaviest bone in the human body?
questions.explanation: The femur, or thigh bone, is recognized as the longest and heaviest bone in the human skeleton, playing a fundamental role in weight-bearing and locomotion.

questions.question: What is the average angle of inclination of the femur in adults?
questions.explanation: The angle of inclination, which is formed between the long axis of the neck and that of the shaft of the femur, averages 125 degrees in adults, and its alteration can lead to conditions such as coxa vara or coxa valga.

questions.question: What is the state in which the angle of inclination of the femur decreases called?
questions.explanation: When the angle of inclination of the femur decreases below normal values, this condition is known as coxa vara, a deformity that affects the biomechanics of the hip joint.

questions.question: What structure of the femur has a depression or fossa where the round ligament is inserted?
questions.explanation: The head of the femur, a little below and behind its center, has a specific depression or fossa where the ligament of the head of the femur, also known as the round ligament, is inserted, essential for its irrigation.

questions.question: What prominent line is found in the middle third of the posterior shaft of the femur?
questions.explanation: The diaphysis of the femur has in its middle third a very prominent posterior edge known as the linea aspera, which has two lips (internal and external) and is a key point for muscle insertions.

questions.question: What is platimeria in the context of the femur shaft?
questions.explanation: Platimeria is the term used to describe excessive flattening of the shaft of the femur in an anteroposterior direction, an anatomical variation that can be observed in imaging studies.

questions.question: What structure inserts into the peripheral edge of the acetabulum and helps deepen the cavity?
questions.explanation: The acetabular labrum, also known as the acetabular labrum, is a fibrocartilaginous structure that is inserted into the peripheral edge of the acetabulum, contributing to deepening the cavity and improving joint congruence with the head of the femur.

questions.question: What part of the hip bone forms approximately two-fifths of the acetabulum?
questions.explanation: The acetabulum is formed by the union of the three parts of the coxal bone: the pubis contributes approximately two fifths, the ilium with almost two fifths, and the ischium with just over two fifths.

questions.question: What is the orientation of the convexity of the femur shaft?
questions.explanation: The shaft of the femur has a distinctive curvature with the convexity facing forward, an anatomical characteristic that gives it strength and helps in the distribution of loads during movement.

questions.question: What rounded prominence is found on the posterior intertrochanteric ridge of the femur?
questions.explanation: The intertrochanteric ridge, which connects the greater trochanter to the lesser trochanter on the posterior surface of the femur, has a rounded elevation known as the quadrate tubercle.

questions.question: What arteries penetrate the shaft of the femur near the linea aspera?
questions.explanation: One or two nutrient arteries are those that penetrate the diaphysis of the femur, located near the linea aspera, and go upward to supply the bone marrow and the compact tissue of the bone.

questions.question: What bone structure of the femur is palpable approximately 10 cm below the iliac crest?
questions.explanation: The greater trochanter, a bony prominence located laterally on the upper epiphysis of the femur, is palpable on the outer aspect of the thigh, approximately 10 cm below the level of the iliac crest, making it an important anatomical landmark.

questions.question: What is the structure that houses the nerve and obturator vessels in the obturator surface of the pubis?
questions.explanation: The obturator aspect of the pubis features the subpubic canal, also known as the obturator sulcus, which is the specific anatomical space designed to accommodate the obturator nerve and vessels as they pass through this region.

questions.question: Which major artery contributes to the vascularization of the head and neck of the femur through internal epiphyseal branches?
questions.explanation: The internal epiphyseal branches, which supply the head and neck of the femur, arise primarily from the obturator artery. These branches are crucial for the blood supply to this bone region.

questions.question: In what week after ovulation does the periosteal collar of the femur appear?
questions.explanation: The ossification process of the femur begins with the appearance of a periosteal collar around the shaft. This pivotal event occurs between the seventh and eighth week after ovulation, marking an early stage of bone development.

questions.question: During what period is the ossification center for the head of the femur observed?
questions.explanation: The ossification center for the head of the femur is one of the first to appear after birth. It is formed specifically during the lactation period, being an important milestone in the bone development of the lower limb.

questions.question: What type of bone is the kneecap based on its shape and function?
questions.explanation: The kneecap, also known as the patella, is classified as a sesamoid bone. This name is due to its inclusion within the tendon of the quadriceps femoris muscle, which allows it to act as a pulley to improve muscle function.

questions.question: With what structure of the femur does the patella articulate on its posterior surface?
questions.explanation: The patella articulates with the distal end of the femur. Specifically, its posterior articular surface is related to the femoral trochlea, forming the patellofemoral joint, essential for knee movement.

questions.question: Which facet of the patella is largest and is used to determine the side of the bone?
questions.explanation: To determine the laterality of the patella, its articular surface is observed. The outer facet is consistently larger than the inner facet, which is a distinctive anatomical feature and a reference point for its orientation.

questions.question: Which of the following is an edge of the patella?
questions.explanation: The kneecap has three main edges: the upper one (known as the base), the inner one and the outer one. In addition, it has a vertex at the bottom. These edges, together with their faces, define the triangular morphology of the bone.

questions.question: Up to what stage is the ossification of the patella complete?
questions.explanation: Ossification of the patella is a process that begins in childhood, from several centers. However, its fusion and the completion of ossification usually extend until puberty or adolescence, consolidating the bone structure.

questions.question: What is the main location of the tibia in the leg?
questions.explanation: The tibia is located in the anterior and inner (or medial) part of the leg. It is a superficial bone that can be easily palpated along its entire length, being the main bone component of the calf and fibula.

questions.question: Which structure of the upper epiphysis of the tibia is expanded to articulate with the femur?
questions.explanation: The upper epiphysis of the tibia is voluminous and expands to form the internal and external condyles, as well as the tibial tuberosity. This configuration allows for proper and stable articulation with the femoral condyles, forming the knee joint.

questions.question: What structures extend proximally from the intercondylar eminence of the tibia?
questions.explanation: The intercondylar eminence of the tibia is a central prominence on the superior articular surface. It extends proximally on each side to form the medial and medial intercondylar tubercles, which contribute to the stability of the knee joint.

questions.question: Which of the following faces does NOT belong to the diaphysis of the tibia?
questions.explanation: The diaphysis of the tibia has three faces: internal, external and posterior. The anterior edge is very prominent and forms the shin, but it is not a face. The faces and edges define the cross section of the long bone.

questions.question: Which edge of the shaft of the tibia is the most prominent and forms the 'shin'?
questions.explanation: The anterior edge of the tibia is the most prominent of its three edges and is what is commonly known as the 'shin'. It is palpable along almost its entire length and is a distinctive feature of the tibia.

questions.question: What structure of the lower epiphysis of the tibia houses the lower part of the fibula?
questions.explanation: The lower epiphysis of the tibia has a wide and deep triangular depression on its external surface, known as the peroneal notch. This notch is specifically designed to accommodate and articulate with the lower part of the fibula.

questions.question: At what time is the ossification center for the superior epiphysis of the tibia usually present?
questions.explanation: The ossification center for the upper epiphysis of the tibia is one of the first to develop. It is usually already present at birth, indicating early and significant bone development in this region.

questions.question: What is a main functional characteristic of the fibula?
options: It is the longest bone in the leg
questions.explanation: Unlike the tibia, the fibula is a thin bone and is not designed to support the weight of the body. Its main function is related to muscle insertion and ankle stability, as well as the formation of the distal tibiofibular joint.

questions.question: What nerve surrounds the head of the fibula, making it vulnerable to injury?
questions.explanation: The external popliteal sciatic nerve, also known as the common peroneal nerve, surrounds the head of the fibula and is located superficially in this area. Because of this anatomical proximity, it is particularly susceptible to compression injuries or direct trauma, which can result in foot drop.

questions.question: How is the appearance of the fibular shaft described in terms of its orientation?
questions.explanation: The diaphysis of the fibula presents a characteristic torsion. It appears to have been bent outward by about a quarter of a right angle. This torsion is relevant to understanding the orientation of the interosseous membrane and the arrangement of the surrounding muscles.

questions.question: Which of the following bony structures forms the external malleolus?
questions.explanation: The lateral malleolus is a prominent bony extension of the inferior epiphysis of the fibula. It is more distal and posterior than the medial malleolus of the tibia, and forms the lateral side of the ankle joint, articulating with the talus.

questions.question: What is a fracture of the lower epiphysis of the fibula associated with ankle dislocations called?
questions.explanation: Fracture of the inferior epiphysis of the fibula, often accompanied by dislocations of the ankle joint, is classically known as Pott's fracture. It is a common type of traumatic injury that affects the ankle and associated ligaments.

questions.question: How many bones make up the tarsus, excluding the metatarsals?
questions.explanation: The tarsus is made up of seven main bones, which are the calcaneus, talus, cuboid, scaphoid (or navicular), and the three cuneiforms (medial, intermediate and lateral). These bones form the proximal portion of the foot.

questions.question: What is the tarsal bone that does NOT have muscular or tendon insertions?
questions.explanation: The talus, also known as the talus, is unique among the tarsal bones in lacking muscle or tendon attachments. Its main function is to transmit the weight of the body from the leg to the rest of the foot, forming a key part of the ankle joint.

questions.question: What prominence of the calcaneus supports the head of the talus?
questions.explanation: The head of the talus rests on a specific prominence of the calcaneus called the Sustainaculum Tali. This bone structure is essential for the support of the talus and contributes to the formation of the medial longitudinal arch of the foot.

questions.question: What arch of the foot is formed by the calcaneus, talus, scaphoid, cuneiforms, and first three metatarsals?
questions.explanation: The inner longitudinal arch of the foot is a key structure for cushioning and weight support. It is formed by the calcaneus, talus, scaphoid, the three cuneiform bones and the first three metatarsals, creating a prominent curve on the medial side of the foot.

questions.question: Which tarsal bone has a prominent tuberosity on its inner surface?
questions.explanation: The scaphoid, also known as the navicular bone, is a tarsal bone distinguished by a prominent tuberosity on its inner surface. This tuberosity is an important anatomical reference point and serves for the insertion of tendons.

questions.question: What anatomical structure has a vertical groove for the tendon of the flexor hallucis longus muscle on its posterior surface?
questions.explanation: The posterior surface of the body of the talus is distinguished by presenting the posterior process, which has a specific vertical groove for the tendon of the flexor hallucis longus muscle, which is then continued in the Sustainaculum tali.

questions.question: What is the calcaneal bone also known as?
questions.explanation: The calcaneus bone, one of the bones of the tarsus, is also commonly known as os calcis. This bone is essential for transmitting much of the body's weight from the talus to the floor.

questions.question: Which of the following bones is located between the talus behind and the three cuneiform bones in front?
questions.explanation: The scaphoid, also called the navicular, is strategically located in the tarsus, articulating posteriorly with the talus and anteriorly with the three cuneiform bones, being a key piece of the arch of the foot.

questions.question: Which tarsal bone is characterized by having a 'calcaneal process' in its inferointernal angle?
questions.explanation: The cuboid bone, located on the external surface of the foot, extends at its inferointernal angle to form a structure called the 'calcaneal process', a distinctive feature of this tarsal bone.

questions.question: Which metatarsal is described as the longest of all?
questions.explanation: The second metatarsal is consistently described as the longest of the metatarsal bones. It extends posteriorly between the internal and external cuneiforms, contributing to the stability of the foot.

questions.question: What is the distinguishing feature of the base of the fifth metatarsal?
questions.explanation: The base of the fifth metatarsal is distinguished by having on its external side the styloid process, also known as the tuberosity. This projects backwards and outwards, being an important anatomical reference point.

questions.question: How many phalanges does the first toe have?
questions.explanation: The first toe, or big toe, has only two phalanges, unlike the other toes which generally have three. These are the proximal phalanx and the distal phalanx.

questions.question: Which of the following tarsal bones usually begins to ossify before birth?
questions.explanation: The three largest bones of the tarsus, which are the calcaneus, talus, and cuboid, usually begin their ossification before birth, with the calcaneus showing periosteal bone around the fourth month of prenatal life.

questions.question: Where does the internal saphenous (great) vein begin?
options: At the junction of the internal dorsal digital vein with the dorsal venous arch
questions.explanation: The internal saphenous vein, the longest superficial vein of the lower limb, originates at the junction of the dorsal digital vein on the inner side of the big toe with the dorsal venous arch of the foot, then passing in front of the internal malleolus.

questions.question: Which vein does the external saphenous vein (saphenous vein) end up draining most often?
questions.explanation: The external saphenous vein ascends along the posterior surface of the leg and, although its termination is variable, it most commonly empties into the popliteal vein in the popliteal fossa.

questions.question: What is the main function of the perforator veins above the ankle?
options: Bring blood from deep to superficial
Direct blood from superficial to deep
questions.explanation: The perforating veins, also known as communicating veins, are equipped with valves that direct blood flow from the superficial veins to the deep veins, especially above the ankle, which is crucial for effective venous return.

questions.question: What artery forms from the anterior tibial artery in front of the ankle?
questions.explanation: The anterior tibial artery is one of the main arteries of the leg. In front of the ankle, this artery continues directly as the pedis artery, also known as the dorsalis pedis, supplying the dorsal region of the foot.

questions.question: Which of the following veins accompanies the internal saphenous nerve?
questions.explanation: The internal saphenous vein, as it travels along the inner surface of the tibia and thigh, is accompanied by the internal saphenous nerve. This anatomical relationship is clinically relevant for venous procedures.

questions.question: What is the structure that can be palpated immediately below the medial malleolus and that forms the middle facet for the talus?
options: The anterior tubercle of the calcaneus
questions.explanation: The Sustainaculum Tali is an extension of the calcaneus that can be palpated immediately below the medial malleolus. Its upper surface forms the middle facet for articulation with the talus.

questions.question: Where are fatigue fractures in the lower limb most frequently found?
questions.explanation: Fatigue fractures, especially those caused by long walks without a history of violence, are a common finding in the metatarsal bones, due to the repetitive stress that these structures endure.

questions.question: Which structure is usually considered an external tubercle separate from the talus when it exists?
options: The anterior process of the calcaneus
questions.explanation: The trigone, or os trigonum, is an accessory ossicle that is considered an external tubercle separate from the posterior process of the talus. It is located in the tubercle of the talus and is one of the most common ossicles.

questions.question: What lymphatic vessels usually empty into the popliteal nodes?
options: The superficial vessels of the calf
The deep vessels that accompany the blood vessels
The lymphatic vessels of the external genitalia
questions.explanation: The main deep lymphatic collecting vessels of the lower limb ascend together with the blood vessels and usually empty into the popliteal nodes, carrying lymph from the most internal structures.

questions.question: What is the artery that passes under the inguinal ligament and changes its name to femoral?
questions.explanation: The external iliac artery is the continuation of the common iliac artery. Once it passes through and beneath the inguinal ligament, it changes its name and is known as the femoral artery, the main artery of the thigh.

questions.question: What two processes or tubercles are observed on the lower surface of the calcaneal tuberosity, on which the bone rests?
questions.explanation: On the lower surface of the calcaneal tuberosity, two processes or tubercles can be observed, the internal tubercle and the external tubercle. These are the support points of the bone, with the internal one being the longest.

questions.question: Which of the following groups of lymph nodes are mostly subcutaneous and palpable in the living subject?
questions.explanation: The inguinal nodes, which vary in number from three to 25, are predominantly subcutaneous and can often be palpated in living individuals, especially superficial ones.

questions.question: Which artery continues as the tibiofibular trunk after giving rise to the anterior tibial artery?
questions.explanation: The popliteal artery, located in the popliteal fossa, has a major bifurcation. It can divide directly into the anterior and posterior tibial arteries, or give rise to the anterior tibial artery and continue as the tibiofibular trunk.

questions.question: Which bone of the foot has a convex articular surface for the scaphoid and a facet for the plantar calcaneoscaphoid ligament?
questions.explanation: The head of the talus is the part of this bone that articulates with the scaphoid. It has a convex articular surface specifically designed for the scaphoid and another facet for the plantar calcaneoscaphoid ligament.

questions.question: In which region of the foot can the cuboid bone be palpated?
questions.explanation: The cuboid bone is a tarsal bone that is located and can be palpated on the outside of the foot. Its position is key for the articulation with the calcaneus and the fourth and fifth metatarsals.

questions.question: What is the important venous anastomosis that is enlarged in cases of obstruction of the superior or inferior vena cava?
options: Between internal and external saphenous vein
Between the abdominal subcutaneous vein and the external mammary vein
questions.explanation: The anastomosis between the abdominal subcutaneous vein (superficial epigastric) and the external mammary vein (via the thoracoepigastric vein) is of great clinical importance, since it can increase in volume significantly in cases of obstruction of the superior or inferior vena cava.

questions.question: What muscle inserts into the two lips of the oblique groove present on the inferior surface of the cuboid?
questions.explanation: The underside of the cuboid is characterized by an oblique groove in its anterior part. On the two lips of this groove, the long plantar ligament is inserted, an important structure for supporting the longitudinal arch of the foot.

questions.question: Which of the following nerves does NOT contribute to the cutaneous innervation of the buttock?
questions.explanation: The skin of the buttock is innervated by the superior, middle, and inferior gluteal nerves, external cutaneous branches of the subcostal and greater abdominogenital nerves, and the perforating cutaneous nerve. The internal saphenous nerve supplies the skin of the leg and thigh, not the buttock.

questions.question: In an intramuscular injection in the gluteal region, which quadrant is considered the safest because it is relatively avascular and free of important nerves?
questions.explanation: The superolateral quadrant of the buttock, as well as the anterior part of the gluteal region (where the tensor fascia lata is located), are areas considered safe for intramuscular injections due to their relative avascularity and absence of important nerves, minimizing the risk of injury.

questions.question: Which muscle is the most superficial of the glutes and forms the main bulge of the buttock?
questions.explanation: The gluteus maximus, medius, and minimus muscles are listed from surface to depth, with the gluteus maximus being the most superficial and primarily responsible for the bulge of the buttock. It is a muscle with thick and powerful fascicles.

questions.question: What is the main nerve that innervates the gluteus maximus muscle?
questions.explanation: The gluteus maximus muscle is innervated exclusively by the inferior gluteal nerve (L5, S1, S2). This nerve penetrates directly into the overlying gluteus maximus muscle to innervate it, being crucial for its thigh extensor function.

questions.question: What is the main action of the gluteus maximus muscle?
questions.explanation: The gluteus maximus is a powerful thigh extensor at the hip joint. It can also extend the pelvis or trunk over the lower limbs when they are fixed, being important in activities such as running or standing up.

questions.question: What nerve innervates the gluteus medius and gluteus minimus muscles?
questions.explanation: The gluteus medius and gluteus minimus muscles are innervated by the superior gluteal nerve (L4, L5, S1). This nerve also innervates the tensor fasciae latae and is crucial for abduction and internal rotation of the thigh, as well as pelvic stability.

questions.question: What is the main action of the gluteus medius and minimus muscles?
questions.explanation: The main actions of the gluteus medius and minimus muscles are the abduction of the thigh and its inward rotation. They are especially important to maintain the stability of the pelvis during walking, preventing it from falling on the side opposite the support.

questions.question: A gluteus medius paralysis manifests clinically as:
options: Loss of sensation in the buttock
questions.explanation: Gluteus medius paralysis produces a characteristic clauding gait, known as a positive Trendelenburg test. In this condition, the pelvis descends on the opposite side of the paralyzed muscle due to the inability of the ipsilateral abductors to counteract gravity, affecting postural stability.

questions.question: What nerve innervates the tensor fasciae latae muscle?
questions.explanation: The tensor fasciae latae muscle is innervated by the superior gluteal nerve, as are the gluteus medius and minimus. This muscle is functionally related to the glutes and thigh flexors, and its innervation comes from the same nerve.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an external rotator muscle of the thigh in the gluteal region?
questions.explanation: The external rotators of the thigh in the gluteal region are piriformis, superior gastrocnemius, obturator internus, inferior gastrocnemius, quadratus cruris, and obturator externus. The gluteus medius, on the other hand, abducts and rotates the thigh inward.

questions.question: What is the main origin of the piriformis muscle of the pelvis?
questions.explanation: The piriformis muscle has its main origin in the pelvic aspect of the sacrum, specifically in the S2 to S4 vertebrae, and also in the greater sacrosciatic ligament. It leaves the pelvis through the greater sacrociatic foramen.

questions.question: What artery leaves the pelvis through the greater sacrosciatic foramen, above the triquetrum?
questions.explanation: The superior gluteal artery, also known as the gluteal artery, is the largest branch of the internal iliac artery and leaves the pelvis through the greater sacrociatic foramen, passing over the piriformis muscle, where it divides into superficial and deep branches.

questions.question: What artery emerges from the pelvis through the greater sacrosciatic foramen, below the piriformis muscle?
questions.explanation: The ischial artery, also known as the inferior gluteal artery, is a branch of the internal iliac artery that runs posteriorly and leaves the pelvis through the greater sacrocyatic foramen, passing under the piriformis muscle, unlike the superior gluteal artery.

questions.question: Which of the following muscles is NOT innervated by the superior gluteal nerve?
questions.explanation: The superior gluteal nerve innervates the gluteus medius, gluteus minimus, and tensor fascia lata muscles, as well as the hip joint. The gluteus maximus, for its part, is innervated by the inferior gluteal nerve, which differentiates them anatomically.

questions.question: Injury to the superior gluteal nerve produces a characteristic motor impairment due to paralysis of which muscle?
questions.explanation: Injury to the superior gluteal nerve causes paralysis of the gluteus medius, resulting in a characteristic limping gait (positive Trendelenburg test), weakening of thigh flexion, and impaired internal rotation. Sensitivity is usually not affected.

questions.question: What muscle is the main innervated by the inferior gluteal nerve?
questions.explanation: The inferior gluteal nerve, which emerges below the piriformis, divides into branches that penetrate and exclusively supply the gluteus maximus muscle. This nerve is essential for the thigh extensor function provided by this muscle.

questions.question: Which nerve of the sacral plexus innervates the skin of the posterior thigh and leg?
questions.explanation: The posterior femoralcutaneous nerve is a cutaneous branch of the sacral plexus (S1 to S3). It descends covered by the gluteus maximus and accompanies the sciatic nerve, being responsible for the sensory innervation of the skin of the posterior surface of the thigh and part of the leg.

questions.question: What are the two main components that make up the sciatic nerve?
options: Tibial nerve and common peroneal nerve
Obturator nerve and inferior gluteal nerve
questions.explanation: The sciatic nerve, the largest nerve in the body, is actually a combination of two different nerves: the internal popliteal (tibial) sciatic nerve and the external popliteal (common peroneal) sciatic nerve, which travel together within a common sheath.

questions.question: What structure is located in front of the sciatic nerve as it travels in the gluteal region?
questions.explanation: The sciatic nerve descends covered by the gluteus maximus. In front, it is successively related to the ischium, the gastrocnemius of the pelvis, the obturator internus and the quadratus cruris. The posterior femoralcutaneous nerve accompanies the sciatic nerve, but is not located anteriorly.

questions.question: Complete injury to the sciatic nerve can cause a foot posture known as:
questions.explanation: A complete injury to the sciatic nerve results in loss of dorsiflexion at the ankle and eversion of the foot, causing the foot to hang in an equinovarus position. This condition is commonly known as 'drop foot' and makes walking difficult.

questions.question: How many main compartments does the fascia lata form in the thigh, due to its intermuscular septa?
questions.explanation: The fascia lata of the thigh inserts into the iliac crest, sacrum, coccyx, and ligaments. Its deep extensions to the femur form the external and internal intermuscular septa, which divide the thigh into three compartments: anterior, posterior and internal, each with its own muscles and neurovascularization.

questions.question: What is the anterior limit of the femoral sheath?
questions.explanation: The femoral sheath, which surrounds the upper part of the femoral vessels, is formed in front by the transversalis fascia and behind by an extension of the iliaca fascia. It contains compartments for the femoral artery and vein.

questions.question: Which of the following structures forms the upper limit of the femoral triangle?
questions.explanation: The femoral triangle is located in the upper third of the anterior surface of the thigh. It is bounded upward by the inguinal ligament, outward by the medial border of the sartorius, and inward by the medial border of the adductor medianus.

questions.question: What structures are primarily contained within the femoral triangle?
options: Saphenous nerve and internal saphenous vein
questions.explanation: The femoral triangle is an important anatomical region that contains the femoral vessels (artery and vein) and the femoral nerve. These structures are essential for the vascularization and innervation of a large part of the lower limb.

questions.question: What structures are contained in the adductor (subsartorial) canal?
options: Femoral vessels and internal saphenous nerve
Femoral nerve and deep femoral vein
questions.explanation: The adductor canal, also called the subsartorial canal, is located in the middle third of the inner thigh and contains the femoral vessels (artery and vein), the internal saphenous nerve, and, generally, the vastus medialis nerve. It is a crucial neurovascular pathway.

questions.question: What is the direct tendon origin of the rectus femoris?
questions.explanation: The rectus femoris originates from a direct or anterior tendon on the anterior inferior iliac spine, and from a reflex tendon on the rim of the acetabulum. These are your main source inserts.

questions.question: What structure is known as the patellar ligament?
questions.explanation: The patellar ligament is the part of the insertion tendon of the rectus femoris of the thigh that is located between the patella and the tibial tuberosity. It is a continuation of the quadriceps tendon that inserts the patella into the tibia.

questions.question: What action does the rectus femoris perform other than extending the leg?
questions.explanation: The rectus femoris, originating from the hip bone (anterior inferior iliac spine and acetabulum), acts as a biarticular muscle. Therefore, in addition to extending the leg, it also flexes the thigh over the hip.

questions.question: Which quadriceps muscle contracts strongly in the last phase of extension and prevents lateral displacement of the patella?
questions.explanation: The vastus medialis is the muscle that contracts most strongly during the last phase of leg extension. Its more horizontal traction helps counteract the tendency of the kneecap to move laterally.

questions.question: What segment of the spinal cord is the center of the knee jerk reflex?
questions.explanation: The patellar reflex is tested by percussing the patellar ligament, which causes a reflex contraction of the quadriceps. The nerve center for this reflex is located in the L3 segment of the spinal cord, which is crucial for its clinical evaluation.

questions.question: Which three tendons form the 'goose's foot' on the inner surface of the tibia?
questions.explanation: The 'goose foot' (pes anserinus) is a common insertion area on the upper and inner surface of the tibia, formed by the tendons of the sartorius, gracilis and semitendinosus muscles. This structure is relevant for its joint function in flexion and internal rotation of the knee.

questions.question: What is the origin of the femoral artery?
questions.explanation: The femoral artery is the direct continuation of the external iliac artery, once it passes under the inguinal ligament. It is the main blood vessel of the thigh and essential for the irrigation of the lower limb.

questions.question: Where is the femoral artery located in the middle third of the thigh?
questions.explanation: In the middle third of the thigh, the femoral artery lies deeper, within the adductor canal (or Hunter's canal). This contrasts with its more superficial location in the femoral triangle in the upper third.

questions.question: Which of the following is a proximal branch of the femoral artery?
questions.explanation: In the proximal part of its course, the femoral artery gives branches such as the abdominal subcutaneous (superficial epigastric), the external iliac circumflex and the external pudendals. The deep femoral and great anastomotic branches are more distal branches in the thigh.

questions.question: What artery gives rise to the circumflex arteries in most cases?
questions.explanation: In approximately half of the cases, the circumflex arteries (external and internal) arise from a common trunk, the deep femoral artery, which in turn is a major branch of the femoral artery. These arteries are crucial for blood supply to the thigh.

questions.question: Which of the following arteries contributes to the cruciform anastomosis?
questions.explanation: The cruciform anastomosis is an important network of vessels around the hip. It is formed by the anastomosis of the first perforating artery (of the deep femoral artery), the ischial artery and the transverse branches of the external and internal circumflex arteries. It is essential to ensure blood flow in case of occlusion.

questions.question: Which of the following veins is a major tributary of the femoral vein?
questions.explanation: The major tributaries of the femoral vein include the deep femoral vein, the internal and external circumflex veins, and the internal saphenous vein. The internal saphenous vein is the longest superficial vein in the body and drains into the femoral vein in the femoral triangle.

questions.question: What is the main segmental origin of the crural (femoral) nerve?
questions.explanation: The crural nerve, also known as the femoral nerve, originates primarily from the L2, L3, and L4 spinal segments of the lumbar plexus. It is the largest branch of this plexus and is essential for the motor and sensory function of the thigh.

questions.question: Which muscle is NOT innervated by the femoral nerve?
questions.explanation: The crural nerve innervates the skin of the anterior and inner thigh, as well as the iliac, pectineus, sartorius, and quadriceps femoris muscles. The adductor magnus is innervated mainly by the obturator nerve and partly by the sciatic nerve.

questions.question: Where does the femoral nerve divide into its terminal branches?
questions.explanation: Upon reaching the femoral triangle, the femoral nerve divides into a number of terminal branches, including cutaneous and muscular branches for innervation of the thigh. This makes it a key structure in that anatomical region.

questions.question: What nerve innervates the skin of the anterior and external aspects of the thigh?
questions.explanation: The external femoral cutaneous nerve, originating from the lumbar plexus, is responsible for the sensory innervation of the skin of the anterior and external surfaces of the thigh. Its involvement can cause paresthesia or pain in this region, known as meralgia paresthetica.

questions.question: Which of the following muscles forms the upper lateral limit of the popliteal fossa?
questions.explanation: The popliteal fossa is a rhombus-shaped region on the back of the knee. Its upper limits are the biceps femoris on the outside (lateral) and the semitendinosus and semimembranosus on the inside (medial).

questions.question: What structures are located posterior to the popliteal artery as it ascends through the popliteal fossa?
options: Popliteal veins and internal popliteal sciatic nerve
questions.explanation: As the popliteal veins ascend through the popliteal fossa, they lie posterior to the artery, and between it and the overlying internal popliteal sciatic nerve. This relationship is important in popliteal fossa surgery.

questions.question: What are the terminal branches of the popliteal artery?
questions.explanation: The terminal branches of the popliteal artery are the anterior and posterior tibial arteries, which originate from the inferior border of the popliteus muscle. These arteries are essential for blood supply to the leg and foot.

questions.question: Which nerve is most susceptible to injury around the neck of the fibula?
questions.explanation: The external popliteal sciatic nerve (common peroneal) is the nerve that is most exposed to injury, especially around the neck of the fibula, due to its superficiality in this area. Injuries can cause 'drop foot' and loss of sensation.

questions.question: What is the main manifestation of an injury to the external popliteal sciatic nerve?
questions.explanation: An injury to the external popliteal (common peroneal) sciatic nerve causes loss of dorsiflexion and eversion of the foot, resulting in the condition known as 'foot drop'. There is also loss of sensation in the dorsum of the foot and the outside of the leg.

questions.question: What type of joint is the hip, according to its morphological classification?
questions.explanation: The hip joint is an enarthrosis (or ball-and-socket joint), meaning it is a multi-axial joint with ball-and-socket joint surfaces that allow a wide range of motion, including flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, and rotation.

questions.question: What is the strongest ligament in the hip joint?
questions.explanation: The iliofemoral ligament is considered the strongest and most important ligament of the hip joint. It is a 'Y' shaped fibrous band that limits hyperextension and is crucial for joint stability, especially when standing.

questions.question: What structure carries the internal epiphyseal vessels to the head of the femur?
questions.explanation: The round ligament (ligament of the head of the femur) is a flattened band that inserts into the fossa of the head of the femur and conducts the internal epiphyseal vessels into it. Although its mechanical importance is doubtful, it is relevant for the vascularization of the femoral head.

questions.question: What is a common cause of avascular necrosis of the head of the femur?
options: Fractures of the neck of the femur
questions.explanation: Fractures of the neck of the femur can injure the epiphyseal vessels that supply the head of the femur, often resulting in avascular necrosis of the head of this bone. This complication is serious and can lead to destruction of the hip joint.

questions.question: Which knee ligament extends from the lateral epicondyle of the femur to the head of the fibula?
questions.explanation: The external collateral (fibular) ligament is a rounded, cord-shaped structure that extends from the lateral epicondyle of the femur to the head of the fibula, reinforcing the outside of the joint.

questions.question: Which nerve has an intimate relationship with the biceps tendon and crosses the popliteus on the outside of the knee?
questions.explanation: The external popliteal sciatic nerve maintains an intimate relationship with the biceps tendon and crosses the popliteus muscle on the external aspect of the knee, being important in the innervation of this region and adjacent structures.

questions.question: What anatomical structure inserts into Gerdy's tubercle, located on the external condyle of the tibia?
questions.explanation: Gerdy's tubercle is a prominence on the lateral condyle of the tibia that serves as an insertion point for the iliotibial band, a fibrous band important for the lateral stability of the knee joint.

questions.question: Which serous bursa of the knee becomes inflamed to produce the condition known as "maid's knee"?
questions.explanation: "Maid's knee" is an inflammation of the prepatellar serous bursa, which is located between the skin and the bottom of the kneecap. It is a common condition due to repetitive trauma or prolonged pressure.

questions.question: With what structure is the internal collateral ligament of the knee directly applied and its deep portion inserted?
questions.explanation: The medial collateral ligament applies directly to the knee joint capsule, and its deep portion inserts into it, into the external aspect of the medial meniscus and into the tibia, providing medial stability to the joint.

questions.question: Which knee ligament specifically limits the backward sliding of the femur on the tibia?
questions.explanation: The anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) extends from the anterior intercondylar area of ​​the tibia to the medial aspect of the lateral condyle of the femur, limiting the rearward sliding of the femur on the tibia, an essential function for knee stability.

questions.question: What anatomical shape does the internal meniscus of the knee joint have?
questions.explanation: The medial meniscus of the knee is described as having a semicircle or C shape, being less mobile than the medial meniscus and attaching to the medial collateral ligament. This shape contributes to its greater susceptibility to tears.

questions.question: Which of the following nerves does NOT contribute to the innervation of the knee joint?
questions.explanation: The knee joint is innervated by fibers from the muscular branches of the crural, internal and external popliteal sciatic, internal saphenous and obturator nerves, but does not receive direct innervation from the ulnar nerve, which innervates the upper limb.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the popliteus muscle in initiating knee flexion from full extension?
questions.explanation: The popliteus muscle is crucial for 'unlocking' the knee joint from a position of full extension and allows displacement of the lateral meniscus, facilitating the initiation of flexion and preventing meniscal injuries.

questions.question: What muscles are mainly responsible for dorsiflexion of the foot?
questions.explanation: The muscles of the anterior region of the leg, such as the tibialis anterior, extensor hallucis longus, and extensor digitorum communis, are primarily responsible for dorsiflexion of the foot.

questions.question: What bones does the tendon of the tibialis anterior muscle insert into?
questions.explanation: The tibialis anterior tendon inserts into the inner surfaces of the first cuneiform and the base of the first metatarsal, contributing to dorsiflexion and inversion of the foot.

questions.question: Which artery is a direct, well-defined continuation of the anterior tibial artery on the dorsum of the foot?
questions.explanation: The pedis artery, also known as the dorsalis pedis artery, is a direct and well-defined continuation of the anterior tibial artery. Its pulsations are palpable between the malleoli, outside the tendon of the extensor hallucis longus.

questions.question: What is a characteristic clinical consequence of anterior tibial (deep peroneal) nerve injury?
questions.explanation: Injury to the anterior tibial nerve causes paralysis of the dorsiflexors and extensors of the fingers, resulting in a 'drop foot' and a 'steppe gait', due to difficulty lifting the foot off the ground during the swing phase.

questions.question: What bone in the foot does the tendon of the peroneus brevis muscle insert into?
questions.explanation: The peroneus brevis tendon surrounds the posterior surface of the lateral malleolus and heads forward to insert into the styloid process of the fifth metatarsal, being an important evertor of the foot.

questions.question: What effect does paralysis of the peroneus longus and brevis due to a musculocutaneous nerve injury produce?
questions.explanation: Injury to the musculocutaneous nerve results in paralysis of the lateral peroneal muscles, leading to loss of foot eversion. This can cause the foot to invert during dorsiflexion and consequently a clubfoot can develop.

questions.question: What muscles mainly make up the structure known as the triceps surae?
options: Calves of the leg and soleus
questions.explanation: The triceps surae is a powerful muscle group located in the calf, composed of the two twin muscles of the leg (gastrocnemius) and the soleus muscle, which join to form the calcaneal (Achilles) tendon.

questions.question: Which nerve innervates all the muscles of the posterior region of the leg?
questions.explanation: All muscles of the posterior region of the leg, both superficial and deep (gems, soleus, plantaris, popliteus, long flexors), are innervated by the internal popliteal sciatic nerve (also called the tibial nerve).

questions.question: In which segment of the spinal cord is the center of the ankle (Achilles) reflex located?
questions.explanation: The ankle reflex, or Achilles reflex, is provoked by percussion of the calcaneal tendon and its neural center is located in the L5 or S1 segment of the spinal cord, evaluating the integrity of these neurological levels.

questions.question: What is the main action of the triceps surae on the foot joint?
questions.explanation: The triceps surae, made up of the gastrocnemius and the soleus, is a powerful plantar flexor of the foot. Additionally, it acts on the inner side of the foot, which tends to invert it. It is essential for walking and jumping.

questions.question: From what bone structure does the rounded, strong tendon of the popliteus muscle originate?
questions.explanation: The popliteus muscle originates from a rounded and resistant tendon in the fossa at the end of the groove on the external face of the external condyle of the femur, occupying this groove during knee flexion.

questions.question: What is the main action of the common flexor digitorum muscle?
questions.explanation: The common flexor digitorum muscle is responsible for flexing the distal phalanges of the last four toes of the foot, being comparable to the deep common flexor digitorum in the upper limb for its action.

questions.question: Behind which malleolus does the tendon of the tibialis posterior muscle descend?
questions.explanation: The posterior tibial tendon descends behind the medial malleolus, where it is supported by the medial annular ligament. It continues forward and inserts into several tarsal bones, being crucial for supporting the longitudinal arch of the foot.

questions.question: How many main muscle compartments are formed in the leg thanks to the intermuscular septa?
questions.explanation: The fascia of the leg, with its extensions that form the anterior and posterior intermuscular septa, divides the leg into three main muscular compartments: anterior (extensor), external (peroneal) and posterior (flexor), each with its own innervation and vascularization.

questions.question: What ligament limits the forward sliding of the femur on the tibia at the knee?
questions.explanation: The posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) extends from the posterior intercondylar area of ​​the tibia to the lateral aspect of the medial condyle of the femur, and therefore limits the forward sliding of the femur on the tibia. Its function is essential for the stability of the knee.

questions.question: What is the largest synovial joint in the human body, according to its joint cavity?
questions.explanation: The joint cavity of the knee is recognized as the largest in the body, due to its large size and the extension of its synovial membrane. This allows it to make wide movements, but also makes it susceptible to strains and fluid accumulations.

questions.question: Which of the following ligaments reinforces the inner aspect of the ankle joint, inserting into the medial malleolus, talus, scaphoid and calcaneus?
questions.explanation: The deltoid ligament, also known as the medial ligament, is a thickening of the capsule on the medial side of the ankle joint, with insertions on the medial malleolus, talus, scaphoid, and calcaneus.

questions.question: What type of joint is the tibiotalar joint, also known as the ankle joint?
questions.explanation: The ankle joint (tibiotalar) is described as a trochlear joint, allowing dorsiflexion and plantarflexion movements between the lower extremities of the tibia and fibula, and the trochlea of ​​the talus.

questions.question: Which of the following muscles is a major plantar flexor of the foot?
questions.explanation: The triceps surae (gems ​​and soleus) and the peroneus longus are the main muscles that plantarflex the foot, according to the description of the muscle movements.

questions.question: What anatomical structures form the strong fibrous union known as the tibiofibular syndesmosis?
questions.explanation: The tibiofibular syndesmosis is a strong fibrous union that is established between the lower extremities of the tibia and the fibula, reinforced by several ligaments such as the interosseous and the anterior and posterior tibiofibular.

questions.question: What is the innervation of the ankle joint?
questions.explanation: The ankle joint is innervated by a combination of nerves, including the tibialis posterior, lateral saphenous and accessory, tibialis anterior and internal saphenous, as well as the tibialis anterior accessory.

questions.question: What ligament joins the sustainculum tali to the scaphoid and completes the cavity of the talolocalcaneonavicular joint?
questions.explanation: The plantar calcaneoscaphoid ligament is responsible for joining the sustainculum tali with the scaphoid, occupying the considerable space between them and completing the talalocalcaneoscaphoid joint cavity.

questions.question: What joints are primarily responsible for inversion and eversion movements of the foot?
questions.explanation: Foot inversion and eversion, movements of the part of the foot distal to the ankle joint, occur primarily at the subtalar and transverse tarsal joints.

questions.question: Which of the following terms describes a congenital deformity where the heel is elevated and directed inward?
questions.explanation: The term 'clubafoot' is used to indicate a specific congenital deformity of the foot in which the heel is elevated (equinus) and directed inwards (varus).

questions.question: What are the bony components that form the internal longitudinal arch of the foot?
questions.explanation: The medial longitudinal arch of the foot is formed by a specific sequence of bones that includes the calcaneus, talus, scaphoid, cuneiforms, and the first three metatarsals.

questions.question: Where is the highest point of the iliac crest approximately located?
questions.explanation: The supracrestile plane, which joins the highest points of both iliac crests, is located at the level of the spinous process of the L4 lumbar vertebra, serving as an important anatomical reference.

questions.question: Which of the following nerves descends through the superior angle of the popliteal fossa and surrounds the neck of the fibula?
questions.explanation: The external popliteal sciatic nerve is described as descending from the sciatic nerve at the upper angle of the popliteal fossa and then surrounding the neck of the fibula to head forward.

questions.question: What artery can be palpated on the dorsum of the foot, usually on the lateral side of the extensor hallucis longus tendon?
questions.explanation: Pediatric artery pulsations are generally palpable on the dorsum of the foot, specifically on the lateral side of the extensor hallucis longus tendon, as an important vascular landmark.

questions.question: Which of these anatomical structures is the highest point of the medial condyle of the femur and can be located following the adductor magnus tendon?
questions.explanation: The third adductor tubercle is identified as the highest point of the medial condyle of the femur, and its location is facilitated by following the adductor magnus tendon downward.

questions.question: What structures form the tarsal canal by creating grooves on its inferior and superior surfaces respectively?
questions.explanation: The tarsal canal is formed by the grooves present on the lower surface of the talus and the upper surface of the calcaneus, separating the subtalar joint from the talarlocalcaneonavicular joint.

questions.question: What foot movement includes supination, adduction, and plantarflexion?
questions.explanation: Foot inversion is described as a complex movement that cannot be performed in isolation, comprising the combination of supination, adduction and plantar flexion, according to the description of foot movements.

questions.question: What is the main distinguishing characteristic of the tarsometatarsal joint of the big toe in humans?
questions.explanation: The tarsometatarsal joint of the big toe is a distinctive characteristic of the human being, and it is highlighted that there may be an opposition movement in it in anthropoids, but not in man.

questions.question: In walking, what is the main role of the hip abductors on the stance side?
questions.explanation: During the stance phase of gait, when weight is carried on one limb, the hip abductors on the stance side contract strongly to act on the pelvis and minimize its tilt toward the free side.

questions.question: At what vertebral level is the tubercle of the iliac crest located?
questions.explanation: The description of the superficial landmarks indicates that the tubercle of the iliac crest is specifically located at the level of the L5 vertebra, being a relevant anatomical reference for the region.

questions.question: What nerve innervates the plantar aspects of the intertarsal joints?
questions.explanation: The plantar aspects of the intertarsal joints are innervated by the plantar nerves, either the internal or external one, while the dorsal aspects are supplied mainly by the tibialis anterior.

questions.question: Which of the following ankle ligaments originates from the malleolar fossa of the fibula and extends to the posterior process of the talus?
questions.explanation: The posterior talofibular ligament is described as a completely independent fibrous band that originates from the malleolar fossa of the fibula and extends to the posterior process of the talus.

questions.question: What structure is located approximately 3 cm below and anterior to the apex of the medial malleolus?
options: Styloid process of the fifth metatarsal
questions.explanation: The tuberosity of the scaphoid is a palpable reference point that is located about 3 cm below and in front of the apex of the medial malleolus, and also about 2 or 3 cm in front of the sustainculum tali.

questions.question: What muscles are responsible for dorsiflexion of the foot?
options: Common flexor and flexor hallucis longus
questions.explanation: The tibialis anterior and the common extensor of the digits are the main muscles that carry out dorsiflexion of the foot, and can be assisted by the extensor hallucis longus.

questions.question: What ligament helps hold the metatarsal heads together and forms a strong holding mechanism for the ball of the foot?
questions.explanation: The deep transverse metatarsal ligament consists of strong transverse fibers that join the plantar ligaments of the metatarsophalangeal joints, helping to hold the metatarsal heads together.

questions.question: What is the position of the line of gravity with respect to the hip joint in the natural standing posture?
options: Passes in front of the hip
questions.explanation: In the natural standing position, the line of gravity descends behind the hip joint, which contributes to the stability of this joint without requiring great muscular activity.

questions.question: What blood vessels and nerves are indicated by a line that begins between the medial malleolus and the heel and ends on the inner surface of the base of the fifth metatarsal?
questions.explanation: The external plantar artery and nerve are indicated by a specific line that begins between the medial malleolus and the heel, and extends to the inner surface of the base of the fifth metatarsal.

questions.question: Which of the following structures is NOT found in the four compartments formed by the internal annular ligament, from front to back?
questions.explanation: The internal annular ligament forms four compartments containing, from front to back: the posterior tibial tendon, the common flexor digitorum tendon, the posterior tibial vessels and nerve, and the flexor hallucis longus tendon. The anterior tibial vessels and nerve do not pass through this tunnel.

questions.question: What is the main action of the tibialis posterior muscle in the foot?
questions.explanation: The tibialis posterior muscle is the main inverter muscle of the foot, meaning it rotates the sole of the foot toward the midline of the body. It also contributes to plantar flexion.

questions.question: Where does the flexor hallucis longus tendon insert?
options: Proximal phalanx of the second finger
Inferior surface of the distal phalanx of the big toe
questions.explanation: The tendon of the flexor hallucis longus follows an oblique course through the sole of the foot and inserts into the underside of the base of the distal phalanx of the big toe. Its main action is to flex this phalanx.

questions.question: What is the most common site to palpate pulsations of the posterior tibial artery?
options: Between the medial malleolus and the calcaneal tendon
Between the lateral malleolus and the calcaneal tendon
On the back of the foot, near the third toe
questions.explanation: Pulsations of the posterior tibial artery are frequently palpable between the medial malleolus and the calcaneal tendon. This is a key point to evaluate peripheral circulation in the lower limb.

questions.question: Which nerve is comparable to the median nerve of the upper limb in terms of its distribution in the foot?
questions.explanation: The internal plantar nerve is comparable to the median nerve in the upper limb due to its muscular and cutaneous innervation in the first toes of the foot and its general distribution in the sole, affecting sensitivity and motor skills.

questions.question: What is the largest artery of the nutritional branches for the long bones, originating from the posterior tibial?
questions.explanation: The posterior tibial artery supplies neighboring muscles and provides a nutrient artery for the tibia, which is the largest of the nutrient arteries for the long bones. This branch is essential for bone vascularization.

questions.question: What is the only muscle present on the dorsum of the foot?
questions.explanation: The pedis, also known as the common extensor digitorum or extensor digitorum brevis, is the only intrinsic muscle of this region of the foot. It is responsible for the extension of the fingers.

questions.question: What nerve supplies the pedis muscle on the dorsum of the foot?
questions.explanation: The pedis muscle, or common extensor digitorum, is the only muscle on the dorsum of the foot and is innervated by the anterior tibial nerve, also known as the deep peroneal nerve. Its function is the extension of the fingers.

questions.question: How many parts is the plantar aponeurosis on the sole of the foot divided into?
questions.explanation: The plantar aponeurosis is a resistant aponeurotic sheet that specializes in the sole of the foot and is divided into three parts: a central portion, an internal portion, and an external portion. These divisions are important for weight support and locomotion.

questions.question: Which of the following tendons is NOT contained in a synovial sheath in front of the ankle, covered by the anterior annular ligament of the tarsus?
options: Tendon of the extensor hallucis longus
questions.explanation: In front of the ankle, covered by the anterior annular ligament of the tarsus, are the synovial sheaths for the tendons of the tibialis anterior, the extensor hallucis longus, and the common extensor digitorum. The flexor hallucis longus tendon is located posterior to the medial malleolus.

questions.question: How many synovial sheaths contain the tendons of the peroneus longus and brevis behind the lateral malleolus?
questions.explanation: Behind the lateral malleolus, a single synovial sheath contains the tendons of the peroneus longus and brevis. This common sheath is subdivided to cover each tendon, allowing them to slide together.

questions.question: Which of these muscles belongs to the first (most superficial) plane of the sole of the foot?
questions.explanation: The most superficial plane of the sole of the foot includes the abductor hallucis, the plantar flexor brevis, and the abductor hallucis. The lumbricals and the fleshy quadrate lie in a deeper plane.

questions.question: What nerve innervates the quadrate cariesus muscle (quadratus plantaris or accessory flexor)?
questions.explanation: The fleshy quadrate muscle is innervated by the lateral plantar nerve. This muscle is important for the action of the long flexor tendons in the sole of the foot, providing powerful traction.

questions.question: Which of the lumbricals of the foot is innervated by the medial plantar nerve?
questions.explanation: The first lumbrical of the foot is innervated by the internal plantar nerve, while the other lumbricals (second, third and fourth) receive innervation from the external plantar nerve. This distribution is analogous to that of the lumbricals of the hand.

questions.question: What nerve innervates the two portions (oblique and transverse) of the adductor hallucis?
questions.explanation: The two portions, oblique and transverse, of the adductor hallucis are both innervated by the external plantar nerve. This muscle is crucial for adduction and flexion of the big toe, as well as maintaining the metatarsal arch.

questions.question: What nerve innervates the dorsal and plantar interosseous muscles of the foot?
questions.explanation: All interossei, both dorsal and plantar, are innervated by the external plantar nerve. These muscles play important roles in flexion of the metatarsophalangeal joints and stability of the arch of the foot.

questions.question: What is an important action of the interosseous muscles of the foot, in addition to flexion of the metatarsophalangeal joints?
questions.explanation: In addition to flexing the metatarsophalangeal joints, the interossei are crucial for strengthening the metatarsal arch, as they originate from and hold the adjacent metatarsals together. They can also adduct and abduct the fingers.

questions.question: What response is seen in the Babinski reflex, indicating a neurological disorder?
options: Slow extension of the big toe and separation from the others
questions.explanation: In the Babinski reflex, stimulation of the sole of the foot produces a slow extension of the big toe and a slight separation of the other toes, indicating an alteration of the motor pathways of the brain and spinal cord.

questions.question: Between which tendons are the pulsations of the pedis (dorsal artery of the foot) usually felt?
options: Extensor digitorum communis and extensor hallucis longus
Flexor hallucis longus and common flexor digitorum
questions.explanation: Pediatric artery pulsations are clinically important and can usually be felt between the extensor hallucis longus tendon and the extensor digitorum communis tendons. This is a key point to evaluate peripheral circulation.

questions.question: Which of the following structures crosses the pediatric artery on its path?
options: External annular ligament of the tarsus
Inferior plate of the anterior annular ligament of the tarsus
questions.explanation: The pedis artery is crossed by the inferior lamina of the anterior annular ligament of the tarsus and by the extensor hallucis brevis along its course. These anatomical relationships are important to understand their location.

questions.question: Which artery is the main formation of the deep plantar arch?
questions.explanation: The plantar arch is formed mainly by the external plantar artery. This artery changes direction at the base of the fifth metatarsal to go inward and form the arch, which is crucial for the vascularization of the sole of the foot.

questions.question: Which muscle of the foot is NOT innervated by the medial plantar nerve?
questions.explanation: The medial plantar nerve innervates the flexor hallucis brevis, abductor hallucis, and first lumbrical. The quadrate fleshy is innervated by the external plantar nerve.

questions.question: What muscle is innervated by the deep branch of the lateral plantar nerve?
questions.explanation: The deep branch of the lateral plantar nerve supplies all the interossei, the lumbricals (except the first), and the adductor hallucis. The abductor digiti minimi and flexor digiti minimi brevis are innervated by their superficial branch.

questions.question: What foot deformity can develop from injury to the tibial nerve in the popliteal fossa, due to the unopposed action of the evertor and dorsiflexor muscles?
questions.explanation: Injury to the tibial nerve in the popliteal fossa can lead to the development of a calcaneovalgus foot. This occurs through the unopposed action of the muscles that perform eversion and dorsiflexion, resulting in a loss of plantarflexion and inversion.

questions.question: What is the cause of pain under the heel in tarsal tunnel syndrome?
questions.explanation: Tarsal tunnel syndrome is characterized by pain under the heel, caused by compression of the posterior tibial nerve where it is covered by the internal annular ligament. This compression affects nerve function.
